description: Crown Castle is a U.S. communications infrastructure REIT focused on leasing shared wireless tower assets to mobile carriers and other network operators.

overview.title: Business Focus
paragraphs: Crown Castle owns and operates one of the largest U.S. portfolios of communications towers, turning physical vertical real estate into recurring lease revenue from wireless carriers. The business sits in the less glamorous but very necessary layer of digital life: the places where everyone’s cloud habits eventually need a radio bolted to steel.

Its historical platform also included small cells and fiber, but recent company materials emphasize a strategic shift toward a more focused tower business after agreements to divest those assets. For registry purposes, small cells should be tracked as a legacy or transition-relevant product area rather than assumed to be part of the future operating mix.

overview.title: Market Role
paragraphs: Crown Castle’s infrastructure supports mobile network coverage, capacity upgrades, and 5G deployment across the United States. Its core customers are wireless carriers that need distributed access to tower locations without owning every site themselves.

The company is best understood as a landlord for telecom infrastructure: it does not sell consumer connectivity, but its assets shape which networks can expand, densify, and compete. That makes it relevant to open-infrastructure analysis even when the product itself is not software.

overview.title: Registry Considerations
paragraphs: The main product entries to validate are Cell Towers and Small Cells, with Fiber flagged for source review because repo context omits it while company disclosures historically tied fiber to small-cell deployment. The 2025 strategic sale announcement is important for deciding whether Fiber and Small Cells belong in current products, legacy products, or corporate-history notes.

Potential alternative mappings should compare Crown Castle’s privately owned shared infrastructure model against municipal broadband, community wireless, open-access fiber, neutral-host networks, and decentralized connectivity projects. The satire writes itself gently: the internet may feel weightless, but rent is still due on the tower.
